title: TBM 401: Solving Problems the Hard Way
questions: How do we define the right number of work lanes (not too vague, not too specific) and assign real ownership?
What routines help teams keep tempo, habits, and focus without over-correcting when drift appears?
How can leaders avoid alignment theater and zombie processes while still using lightweight planning artifacts?
How should lanes change over time—when to refactor, pivot, or retire them?
summary: This post argues that effective leadership and strategy execution come from shaping a small set of durable “lanes” with clear ownership, weekly calibration, and habit/tempo coaching—because drift and uncertainty are inevitable. It’s aimed at leaders and team members who want practical operating routines that avoid alignment theater and instead build real learning through collaborative discovery and ongoing refactoring. The piece also critiques overly rigid, frameworkified versions of agile and reframes planning as a continuous sensemaking practice across organizational levels.

title: TBM 395: Words! Damned Words!
questions: When do different definitions of the same work noun (initiative, bet, program) become an actual organizational problem?
How can we reduce semantic drift and ontological conflict without forcing everyone into one shared vocabulary?
What happens when governance and accounting models mistake abstractions for reality in product development?
How should we decide what to coordinate and fund across the org when boundaries between concepts are fuzzy?
search_terms: initiative vs bet vs project definitions
summary: This post explores how organizational work nouns (like initiatives, bets, and capabilities) can either help coordination or cause failure when meanings collide across strategy, finance, and delivery. It’s for product and organizational leaders who care about language, governance, and the practical mechanics of prioritization—especially when semantic ambiguity affects incentives, funding, and decision-making. The author argues for grounding alignment in events and designing systems that tolerate multiple frames rather than enforcing one “correct” definition.

title: TBM 393: Why Labeling Relationships Is So Important
questions: How should goals and initiatives relate in an operating model (anchor, derived, constrained, or aggregated)?
What worldview is encoded by different strategy-to-execution models (linear decomposition vs learning-loop bets)?
Why do hierarchical scaling approaches like SAFe-style rollups or cascading OKRs fail at enterprise scale?
How can mapping an organization as a semantic network or lightweight ontology improve understanding of work?
search_terms: strategy to execution bets vs commitments
summary: The post argues that organizations should be modeled by explicitly labeling the relationships between work concepts (edges carry meaning), not by relying on tidy hierarchies. It helps leaders and strategy practitioners compare competing mental models for how goals and initiatives connect, and it explains why scaling agile and goal cascades often break due to changing dynamics at larger distances. The intended audience is people designing or diagnosing operating models who want better conceptual tools for mapping how work actually flows.

title: TBM 383: Maximizers vs. Focusers
questions: Why do maximizers and focusers keep misunderstanding each other at work?
How should leaders balance optionality and parallel bets with focus and coherence?
What does it mean to treat strategy as something other than a retrospective exercise?
How can organizations use multiple archetypes without letting their risks dominate?
summary: This post explains why leaders and teams get stuck in recurring conflicts between “maximizers” (speed, breadth, optionality) and “focusers” (constraints, coherence, depth). It argues that these differences are bias-driven rather than skill-driven, and proposes a framework for healthy organizations that preserve multiple archetypes while damping their failure modes. It’s aimed at leaders and strategists trying to improve execution and alignment when teams chase too much or do too little.

title: TBM 379: Vertical vs. Horizontal Org Coupling
questions: How should we think about vertical vs. horizontal coupling in our org?
When do dense cross-team dependencies require command-and-control versus other coordination mechanisms?
Why do organizations drift from federated islands into gridlock, and how can that cycle be interrupted?
How does cohesion change the trade-offs between coupling levels?
summary: The post offers a 2x2 mental model for thinking about organizational coupling—vertical cohesion versus horizontal dependencies—and how “control” relates to coupling rather than direction alone. It’s aimed at leaders and org designers trying to diagnose why companies drift into gridlock and how to choose coordination patterns based on cost, cohesion, and the organization’s needed direction. It also argues that startups and enterprises often struggle with opposite coupling problems, so the “right” box is context-dependent.

title: TBM 376: Why Are We Organized Like This?
questions: Which org design pattern best matches how my company is organized today?
Are our re-orgs driven by real strategic needs or by buzzword fads and internal politics?
Do we change in pendulum swings or through gradual, empowered transformation?
How do founder influence and management discipline shape our ability to adapt to new domains like AI?
search_terms: why companies are organized like this
summary: This post presents nine recognizable organizational design “patterns” and helps leaders and managers diagnose why their company’s structure looks the way it does. It frames org design as an outcome of power dynamics, scaling history, decision-making habits, and founder influence—then contrasts survival/thrive/hack moves for each pattern. It’s aimed at people trying to interpret their company’s reorg behavior and improve adaptability, especially when facing new challenges like AI.

title: TBM 271: Diagnosing Your Company's Strategy Problem
questions: Why can’t our company converge on a strategy even when we have smart people?
Where is our insight gap—are we flying without radar or suffering from fabricated certainty?
Do we actually agree on the game and the options, or are we stuck in narrative clashes and factional risk profiles?
What prevents us from committing to a 'now what' when information is incomplete and every option has trade-offs?
search_terms: investor incentives vs founder incentives strategy
summary: This post offers a practical framework for leaders and operators to diagnose why a company seems strategically stuck. It argues that weak strategy often reflects failures across three pillars—information and insight coherence, shared clarity on the game/options, and realism about the 'now what' under incentives and incomplete knowledge—rather than a simple lack of strategic thinking. It is aimed at managers, directors, and executives who need to identify which gap is driving indecision and how to move toward focused commitments.

title: TBM 367: The Wicked "Big Picture" Loop
questions: How do we prevent the big picture loop where simplified dashboards drift from reality?
What should leaders do when teams are told to simplify but reality keeps getting hidden?
How can we design lightweight constraints that surface hot spots without killing autonomy?
What accountability cadence helps teams close loops instead of back-channeling?
search_terms: OKR cycle missed because of simplification
how to stop optics game leadership
summary: The post explains a recurring organizational failure mode where executives repeatedly ask for a simplified “big picture,” teams filter reality to comply, and leadership eventually disengages until a crisis forces the truth back into view—then the cycle restarts. It is aimed at product and leadership teams trying to improve roadmap/OKR execution by designing better constraints and accountability mechanisms (including coaching-like cadence) to keep planning aligned with reality.

title: TBM 365: The Problem With Value Hierarchies (Video)
questions: Why do value hierarchies (pillars to tasks) fail to represent real strategy and learning?
How can teams model both outcomes and execution across different time horizons?
What artifact types and cadences best support a networked operating model?
Which rituals help resolve contradictions across strategy, finance, structure, and work lenses?
summary: This post critiques the common “value hierarchy” pyramid used for strategy-to-task planning, arguing it misrepresents how information, outcomes, and time-to-impact actually flow in organizations. It’s aimed at leaders and product/strategy teams designing operating models, proposing a multi-lens, networked approach using artifact types (anchor/intent/context) and rituals to keep the system coherent over time.

title: TBM 353: Conversation Quality and Scale
questions: How mature are our conversations and interactions, and what level of reflexive integration are we actually operating at?
Where should we focus to improve conversation quality, and is that focus strategic rather than just aspirational?
Are we confusing autonomy with isolation, leading to coordination failures between teams?
Are our conversation problems primarily skill gaps, or are they driven by incentives, trust, feedback, fear, or missing environmental support?
summary: This post proposes a way to assess the “maturity” of organizational conversations and interactions, treating them as signals of an organization’s operating system. It’s aimed at leaders and practitioners who want to diagnose conversation quality, decide where to focus, and understand how scale, integration, and scaffolding affect whether good local interactions become reliable org-wide coordination. It also argues that conversation gaps are often systemic (feedback, goals, trust, incentives, fear, environment) rather than purely training problems.

title: TBM 348: Shared Understanding At Scale
questions: How can we make high-volume organizational information legible and actionable for leaders?
What views or dashboards should we build if there is no single universal big picture?
Where are our goal trees, roadmaps, or loops over-nested and producing “worthless” middle layers?
Which models are shaping what gets valued and resourced in our organization—and what do they leave out?
summary: This post explores how organizations can create shared understanding at scale by improving the way information is structured, filtered, and presented to decision-makers. It argues against universal dashboards and over-nested goal/loop structures, emphasizing task-coupled, role-specific views and ritualized shared interfaces. It also warns that common frameworks and models compress reality in ways that can distort power, attention, and resourcing—useful for leaders, product/strategy teams, and org designers.

title: TBM 347: "Why Don't Our Leaders Care About How We Work?"
questions: Why do leaders resist caring about how people work?
Is operating system design only possible in rare, founder-led cases?
How can I get leadership to take a more intentional approach to ways of working?
What should I do when chiefs of staff or program managers try to redesign the company too quickly?
search_terms: why leaders don't care about how we work
middle-out push fails global vs local
chief of staff redesigns operating model
summary: This post addresses why leadership teams often resist intentional design of a company’s ways of working, even when they value accountability and agency. It’s aimed at practitioners trying to influence operating system design inside organizations—especially when leaders treat “process” as beneath them, lack the relevant skill set, or delegate OS work to chiefs of staff too quickly. The guidance focuses on behavior-based framing, storytelling, partnership, and accounting for inertia and local autonomy.

title: TBM 344: Context, Possibility, Intent, and Action
questions: What rituals, artifacts, and cadences should we use to align possibility, intent, action, and context?
Where is our company getting the intent mix wrong across time horizon, procedural vs outcome orientation, concreteness, and leverage?
How should we adjust control and integration mechanisms as we move from startup scale to larger-company scale?
What should we do when context fragmentation leads to incoherent decisions and “not strategic enough” blame?
search_terms: team operating system possibility intent action context
intent types short-term long-term procedural outcome concrete abstract high leverage
control and integration cadence for organizations
portfolio of bets for future growth
summary: The post proposes a practical “operating system” lens for teams and organizations, built from five elements: possibility, intent, action, context, and control/integration. It argues that strategy and execution fail when intent signifiers are mixed incorrectly, and it offers a spectrum of intent types to add nuance beyond generic planning cascades. It’s aimed at leaders and operators designing rituals, artifacts, and cadences—especially when scaling from startup simplicity to larger-company complexity.

title: TBM 326: Adhocracies and Bureaucracies
questions: How do adhocracies accumulate implicit bureaucracy even when they claim to avoid process?
Why do attempts to eliminate bureaucracy sometimes create more bureaucracy or power-oriented dynamics?
What organizational typologies can coexist inside the same company, and how does that affect collaboration?
How should enterprises approach transformation when their starting point is centralized bureaucracy?
search_terms: integrative roles program managers centralized ops platform teams
summary: The post contrasts adhocracies in rapid-growth tech (especially post–0-1) with bureaucracies in large enterprises, arguing that process-phobia often turns into implicit bureaucracy. It’s aimed at leaders and product/ops practitioners navigating scaling, transformation, and cross-team execution, offering a framework for understanding cycles of constraint, integrative “glue” roles, and organizational typology shifts.

title: TBM 319: Surfing Inertia and Pendulum Swings
questions: Why do organizations experience harsh pendulum swings between independence and coordination?
Is organizational inertia inevitable, and are leaders powerless against it?
How can companies dampen disruptive restructuring without relying on silver bullets?
What does it mean to build a dynamic capability for org-wide adaptation?
summary: The post argues that many organizations claim to be agile while operating in systems that amplify inertia, producing repeated swings between team independence and centralized coordination. It is aimed at leaders and practitioners designing operating models who want practical ways to dampen disruptive restructuring through sensemaking, incentive alignment, temporary scaffolding, and normalized adaptation. The core focus is building an adaptive capability to monitor the organization’s trajectory and adjust the independence/coordination balance over time.

title: TBM 309: Zombie Practices and Processes
questions: Why do OKRs fail in large companies even when they work in small teams?
How does a practice drift from its original purpose into a zombie process?
What incentives and organizational dynamics cause OKRs to become performance-management paperwork?
What should leaders do to fight mission drift and restore usefulness to teams?
summary: This post explains why OKRs (and similar management practices) often degrade into “zombie processes” in large organizations, despite being straightforward in theory. It is aimed at product, engineering, and leadership teams trying to restore strategic focus and alignment by addressing mission drift, incentive misalignment, and the behavioral conditions that make practices stick or fail.

title: TBM 308: Boundary of Safely Challenged Assumptions
questions: Why is it harder for software teams to talk about human factors and the system when performance deteriorates?
What prevents software organizations from challenging assumptions during incoherence rather than blaming people or tightening process?
How does low visibility and unclear cause/effect in software affect learning and adaptation?
What organizational size and structure best supports systematic introspection under non-ideal conditions?
summary: This post argues that software product organizations idealize mission command, autonomy, and small-team heroics, but lack the vocabulary and mechanisms to challenge system assumptions when things go wrong. It contrasts software’s low visibility and hard-to-trace cause/effect with manufacturing’s more consistent shop-floor problem solving, and proposes designing org boundaries around the ability to safely challenge assumptions under non-ideal conditions. It’s for leaders and practitioners in software product development who want more robust organizational learning and adaptation during incoherence.

title: TBM 303: Official, Real, and the Ideal
questions: How does my company balance official processes, real day-to-day work, and the effort to translate between them?
Where do we engage in compliance theater or box-checking that fails to help frontline teams?
When we claim things are “fine” or “just work,” are we describing reality or selling an optics-driven ideal?
How can we spread local team best practices without denying teams’ reality or creating new heavy formal dependencies?
summary: The post proposes a five-part lens for understanding how organizations juggle official processes, real work, the translation effort between them, optics-driven idealizations, and well-meaning improvement directions. It’s aimed at leaders and managers who want to diagnose why “everything is fine” narratives persist, especially around compliance, dashboards, and dependency-heavy processes. It also warns that genuine change can be mistaken for—or turn into—another form of idealization that denies teams’ lived reality.

title: TBM 294: Close the Loop. Stop the Drift
questions: What should we be stubborn about to ensure we don’t let problems fester or drift over time?
How can we build shared understanding of the signals that things are slipping?
Where are our processes running us instead of helping us execute and course-correct?
What dissonance or open loops are we avoiding, and how can we close them earlier?
search_terms: close the loop on open questions
team drift and how to stop it
summary: This post argues that high-performing teams stay effective by actively closing feedback loops: revisiting open threads, integrating perspectives, surfacing dissonance, and escalating early when things break. It is aimed at engineering/product leaders and team members who want practical ways to prevent execution drift, avoid optics-driven incentives, and maintain course-correction under change.

title: TBM 287: Layoffs, Pendulums, Layers, and Gemba Walks
questions: What tactics help companies tame severe organizational pendulum swings during growth and contraction?
How can leaders sense and respond to organizational incoherence early despite increasing noise and cascades?
What is the right balance between local autonomy and global coordination to prevent complexity from compounding?
How should leaders engage with front-line reality without undermining trust and empowerment?
summary: The post argues that layoffs and complexity often follow predictable patterns created during rapid growth, when incentives favor local independence and duplication while global coordination lags. It explores how leaders can detect early organizational incoherence despite rising noise and cascades, and proposes Lean gemba walks plus cross-cutting accountability (in the spirit of the chief engineer) as a way to reduce overshoot during scaling. It is aimed at product, engineering, and operations leaders trying to redesign operating systems for dependency-heavy environments.

title: Sociotechnical Maestros with Gene Kim
questions: How do you distinguish simplification from the trap of oversimplification in complex organizations?
What organizational wiring changes create real independence of action instead of empowerment theater?
When should teams decouple (more modularity) versus re-couple (restore locality) to reduce coordination overhead?
How can leaders sustain long-term transformation when people and teams have short time horizons?
search_terms: devops enterprise technology leadership summit themes
summary: The post is an interview-style discussion of Gene Kim’s “Wiring the Winning Organization,” aimed at leaders and practitioners trying to improve organizational performance through sociotechnical design. It uses metaphors like moving a couch to explain how coherence and coupling affect coordination costs, and it connects these ideas to DevOps, platforms/APIs, and lean-style continuous improvement. The conversation emphasizes enabling independence of action via architectural rewiring and using incremental experimentation to keep the organizational “wiring” working as complexity grows.

title: Octopus Careers & Throwaway Stickies with Chris Butler
questions: How do you find your “tribe” inside large companies when your interests don’t fit a single career track?
What’s the difference between strategy and a plan or roadmap, and why does it matter?
How can leaders introduce new collaboration and decision-making practices without triggering cultural backlash?
Why do some cultures demand perfect documentation and consensus, and how does that affect strategic rehearsal?
search_terms: critical uncertainties two by two scenario planning
summary: This post is an interview focused on how product operations and product leaders can drive change inside large organizations while preserving their own multi-disciplinary identity. It’s for practitioners who want practical ways to rehearse strategy under uncertainty (e.g., Wardley mapping, design fiction, scenario planning) and to build feedback-rich team cultures without micromanaging. The discussion contrasts consensus-driven corporate cultures with communities of practice that treat artifacts as disposable learning tools.

title: TBM 243: Capital Inefficiency
questions: Is our focus on developer productivity really about performance, or is it masking capital inefficiency?
Where are we overstaffed or understaffed relative to market opportunity, architecture, and feedback systems?
How did low interest rates shape our past decisions, and what should we do differently now?
Why do productivity and cost-cutting conversations become blame and identity battles instead of problem-solving?
summary: The post argues that “productivity” and “efficiency” debates in tech are often surface-level proxies for deeper capital inefficiency in how companies fund, staff, and structure work. It’s aimed at leaders and practitioners who are navigating layoffs, shifting interest-rate realities, and stakeholder blame dynamics, and it recommends using systems thinking to address capital efficiency across org design, underinvestment, and feedback/enablement—not just headcount or near-term ROI.

title: TBM 229: Winnable and Unwinnable Games (Part 3)
questions: How can organizations prevent information from degrading as it moves up and down the hierarchy?
When does a hierarchical execution process become an 'unwinnable game,' and how can it be redesigned?
What would good feedback loops and incremental progress signals look like in a multi-team engineering org?
How can leaders preserve meaningful choices and agency while still managing risk and constraints?
search_terms: winnable vs unwinnable games organizational design
engineering org planning scope cut incentives
good game criteria for process design
summary: This post uses a game metaphor to analyze how hierarchical engineering organizations can fail to solve complex problems when information is repeatedly simplified, incentives misalign, and feedback arrives too late. It is aimed at leaders and practitioners who want to redesign execution processes so goals stay clear, agency remains meaningful, and teams get timely signals of progress. The discussion maps the scenario against a set of 'good game' criteria to identify where the system becomes unwinnable and how to improve it.

title: TBM 229: Winnable and Unwinnable Games (Part 1)
questions: How do you tell when your team is playing an unwinnable game?
What changes (drivers, limiting constraints, floats, enabling constraints) make a work system winnable?
How should you introduce a “right” way of working when trust and psychological safety are low?
How do you resolve clashes between different games played by leaders, managers, and engineers?
search_terms: drivers limiting constraints floats enabling constraints
say do commitment scope deadline far in future
low trust environment agile proxy metrics
North Star Framework autonomy meaningful goals
summary: This post reframes product development and delivery frameworks as “games” that can be designed to feel winnable, motivating, and valuable to customers and the business. It explores why teams end up in unwinnable or low-value games—especially when constraints, drivers, and trust dynamics are misaligned—and how multiple organizational “games” clash across roles. It’s aimed at product leaders, engineering managers, and teams trying to improve delivery outcomes by redesigning the work system.

title: TBM 205: "Process" vs. Systems & Habits
questions: How do we distinguish valuable ways of working from performative, non-value-add process?
What incentives cause teams to fall into reactive planning and learned helplessness?
How can leaders build habits and systems that enable autonomy while staying aligned to company strategy?
Why do big-bang offsites fail to sustain improvements after returning to business as usual?
search_terms: how to break a wicked loop in organizations
summary: The post contrasts two organizational approaches to planning: a compliance-driven, annual “process” that produces performative rushing versus a habit-based “system” that continuously shapes ideas and keeps roadmaps aligned. It’s aimed at leaders and teams trying to improve strategy execution, morale, and adaptability by replacing reactive governance with human-centric operating habits and incremental change.

title: TBM 46/52: (F)acts, (A)ssumptions, (B)eliefs
questions: How can our team quickly get aligned on the foundational premises behind our work?
Which of our assumptions are we operating on now versus testing for the future?
How do we track and update facts, assumptions, and beliefs over time without losing context?
What should we do when everything seems to change all the time?
summary: The post proposes a lightweight team exercise—facts, assumptions, beliefs (FAB)—to improve alignment on the underlying premises driving day-to-day work. It’s aimed at teams and leaders who want a practical way to distinguish operating assumptions from hypotheses to test, assign confidence levels, and create a recurring review ritual with historical context. It also extends the idea to both company-wide and team-level alignment (fractal FAB).

title: TBM 40/52: Drivers vs. Fixers
questions: What should I do when I hit a roadblock: push forward pragmatically or fix the underlying mess?
How do incentives and rewards cause drivers and fixers to reinforce each other’s worst patterns?
Why do teams get stuck in exclusion and mono-culture between operators and thinkers?
How should leaders structure teams so drivers and fixers can partner effectively?
summary: This post contrasts two organizational mindsets—drivers who optimize for near-term forward progress and fixers who focus on untangling underlying problems. It argues that when one mindset dominates, self-reinforcing loops of exclusion, stereotypes, and mono-culture emerge, harming both progress and retention. The piece is aimed at leaders and team builders who need to design incentives and team structures that let drivers and fixers partner productively.

title: TBT 35/52: The Benefits of Stubborn Consistency
questions: What do we value, and do team members clearly understand what “good” looks like?
Are our practices culturally dependent, and do we apply them consistently enough to create predictability?
Are we opinionated and coherent—or incoherent and maladaptive?
If conditions change, will we update beliefs and try new ideas with enough curiosity?
summary: The post argues that team and organizational success depends less on any single engineering practice and more on coherence, intentionality, and consistent cultural signaling. It uses a pair-programming example to show how stubborn conviction can be effective when paired with fairness and the ability to update beliefs as conditions change, and it challenges leaders to examine what they value and how they model “good.”

title: TBM 31/52: Why Does Our Strategy Keep Changing?
questions: Why does our strategy keep changing every six months?
Are we confusing tactics with strategy?
What biases or dynamics (like recency bias or premature convergence) are driving our yo-yo strategy?
Do we have a stable strategic core, or are we layering changes without a consistent diagnosis?
search_terms: elephant in the room strategy problem
summary: This post helps leaders diagnose why their organization’s strategy feels reactive and keeps changing, often creating “yo-yo” cycles and operational whiplash. It frames common failure modes—like tactics masquerading as strategy, recency bias, premature convergence, unresolved core issues, and watered-down strategy—and encourages teams to identify which root causes are present. It’s for executives and strategy teams seeking more stable strategic clarity and better decision-making around what to change and what to hold constant.

title: TBM 30/52: Why Don’t We Have a Strategy?
questions: Why do organizations reward reaction and output more than strategic thinking?
What’s missing when teams treat goals and pillars as “strategy”?
How do diagnosis, kernel, and guiding policies improve strategy resilience?
How does private strategy knowledge affect learning and plan revision?
search_terms: why we don't have a strategy
good strategy bad strategy coherent actions
productivity output rituals vs thinking time
summary: The post argues that the real barrier to strategy is not people’s inability to think, but organizational incentives and constraints that make strategic discourse and contemplation scarce. It uses Rumelt’s framework to distinguish strategy (diagnosis, kernel, guiding policies, and plausible immediate actions) from goal/pillar theater, and explains how keeping strategy private can trap learning in a small circle. It’s aimed at product and organizational leaders who want to build a culture where strategic thinking is practiced and shared.

title: TBM 18/52: We Need Someone Who Has Done "It" Before
questions: Why do we keep failing to hire for the same role and end up with a revolving door?
How can leaders define “it” when they can’t explain the context or the underlying playbooks?
When should we copy, mix, adapt, or invent playbooks for a new hire to succeed?
How can we use wardley mapping to clarify hiring needs and reduce misalignment?
search_terms: hire someone who has done it before
copy and paste vs invent playbooks
summary: This post addresses why “hire someone who has done it before” often produces repeated failures and revolving-door turnover. It argues that the real issue is leaders and candidates operating with mismatched implicit knowledge about what “it” actually entails, and it proposes classifying work into playbook types. For leaders, hiring managers, and org designers, it recommends using wardley mapping to visualize the capability network and to guide both hiring conversations and candidate evaluation.

title: TBM 15/52: A Tale of Two Meetings
questions: Why do some meetings produce action items that never get executed?
What structural constraints cause teams to drift into inefficient planning and committee work?
How can an organization redesign meetings to improve decision traceability and follow-through?
What metrics indicate whether change efforts are actually improving execution?
search_terms: meeting effectiveness next steps not happening
how to create more execution meetings
summary: The post contrasts two meeting archetypes to explain why some teams repeatedly fail to translate brainstorming into executed next steps. It argues that meeting dysfunction is often structural—driven by dependencies, WIP, outdated roadmaps, and information/ownership gaps—leading to a drift into failure. Intended for leaders and product/engineering teams seeking practical ways to shift energy and information flow so more decisions turn into real execution.

title: TBM 44/52: Three Teams (Boring, Chaotic, Buffered)
questions: How can an organization create conditions for stable, effective teams rather than chaotic or merely buffered ones?
What’s the difference between buffering a team and actually achieving outcomes tied to the big picture?
How should teams maintain a coherent narrative from insights to outcomes across quarters?
How do executive direction changes and constraint pressure affect team control and reflection time?
search_terms: executive swoop and poop leadership cascades
summary: The post compares three types of teams—boring (stable and effective), chaotic (reactive and directionless), and buffered (protected but still affected by upstream dysfunction). It’s aimed at leaders and operators trying to understand how organizational structure, executive behavior, and team narratives influence the path from insights to outcomes. The core takeaway is that buffering chaos isn’t enough; teams need stability plus coherent alignment to the big picture to achieve sustained results.

title: TBM 17/52: Decision Making vs. Decision Understanding
questions: Why do teams keep going back to the drawing board after they think they decided?
How can we reduce decision drift when uncertainty and context keep changing?
What’s the difference between decision making and decision understanding in real team work?
When do goals, OKRs, and RACI help versus when do they become harmful proxies?
summary: This post argues that team problems labeled as “decision making” are often actually problems of “decision understanding,” where shared vocabulary, assumptions, and implications fail to converge. It’s aimed at leaders and team members working on complex, cross-disciplinary work who are trying to prevent decision drift without relying solely on planning frameworks, goals, or role assignment charts.

title: TBM 15/52: Stop Hiring Things to Do Too Many Jobs
questions: Are we overburdening this framework, tool, or label with too many purposes?
Do new team members understand the nuance and intent behind our operating artifacts?
How can we tell whether our OKRs or segmentation shorthand is creating alignment or confusion?
What test can we run to measure whether our internal messaging is convergent?
search_terms: stop hiring things to do too many jobs
anti-pattern frameworks doing too many jobs
summary: This post argues that adopting a framework, tool, or label to serve too many different purposes creates confusion, semantic drift, and weak alignment—especially for new joiners. It uses OKRs and a customer-segment label as examples and proposes a simple diagnostic: ask multiple people why you use the artifact and check how convergent their answers are.

title: TBM 51/53: Coherence > Shallow Autonomy
questions: How do you empower teams without falling into shallow autonomy?
What does it mean to frame a coherent strategy that enables creativity and agency?
How much should leadership be opinionated, and at what level?
What kind of context do passionate team members actually need to stay aligned?
summary: The post argues that effective empowerment requires leaders to provide a coherent, evidence-backed, and appropriately opinionated strategy—not just vague vision statements or overly prescriptive planning. It is aimed at leaders and product/strategy practitioners who want to enable creativity and agency while keeping teams aligned and avoiding mediocrity. It also challenges the assumption that autonomy always means bottom-up planning.

title: TBM 41/53: Strategy and Structure
questions: Is our strategy implicit or explicit, coherent, and plausible?
Does our organization design actually support the strategy, or are we just mapping teams to tasks?
When the environment shifts, can our strategy and org design keep up and adapt?
How can we restructure continuously and safely instead of relying on big-bang changes?
summary: This post offers a set of diagnostic questions for leaders and facilitators trying to understand why strategy changes don’t translate into real progress. It argues that failure often comes from treating strategy and structure as the same thing, leaving the old implicit strategy and org design in place while the environment shifts again. It’s aimed at teams navigating leadership transitions, strategy pivots, and the need for adaptive organizational design.

title: TBM 37/53: Talk to Your Manager, Take the Survey
questions: Why does feedback from engagement surveys and managers fail to reach the right level of the organization?
When should organizations use surveys versus retrospectives for organizational learning?
How can we reduce bias and filtering when feedback needs to travel across functions?
What feedback mechanisms work best for remote teams to avoid siloed communication?
search_terms: why employee feedback doesn't lead to action
summary: This post argues that when feedback needs to address non-local, cross-functional issues, relying on manager-to-top escalation and engagement surveys often fails due to bias, representativeness problems, and lack of trust in follow-through. It is aimed at leaders and product/software teams looking for better organizational learning mechanisms—especially large-scale retrospectives and continuous improvement events—to counter siloed communication in remote work.

title: TBM 11/53: The Know-It-All CEO
questions: How do we improve remote team performance when advice is everywhere but progress stalls?
What should we change when a know-it-all CEO’s top-down roadmap stops working as the product scales?
How do we decouple decision-making authority from the mechanisms for learning from decisions?
What mechanisms help front-line teams trace their work to strategy and the why?
search_terms: UX and technical debt organizational causes
summary: The post is for leaders and change agents dealing with scaling challenges in product organizations—especially when decision-making becomes overly centralized around a highly knowledgeable CEO. It argues that remote teams and growing companies need mechanisms for inspection and adaptation, making bets visible, and running decision reviews, rather than focusing on who has authority to decide. The recommended action is to decouple decision-making approach from bet visualization and outcome reflection.
